primary.usage: Signature storefront blue — awning, exterior paint, brand recognition color

secondary.usage: Cream/golden accents — headers on Tumblr blog, warm highlight for headings

accent.usage: Deep red/burgundy — interior wall color, warm accent for vintage feel

warmGold.usage: Amber/gold — antique jewelry tones, button highlights, price tags

main.usage: Warm cream page background — matches the eclectic warmth

heading.usage: Espresso dark — rich, warm heading text

body.usage: Warm brown body text — earthy, vintage feel

colorPalette.overallTheme: warm-eclectic — light cream base with rich pops of signature blue, burgundy, and amber/gold accents; bohemian vintage market feel

headingFont.style: bold industrial-vintage — matches their Tumblr blog font exactly

bodyFont.style: clean modern readable — balances the strong heading font

brandVoice.tone: warm-casual — like a friend who's travelled the world and brought back treasures to share

messagingPatterns.headlines: Short, evocative — naming what you'll find rather than what they are
messagingPatterns.descriptions: Story-driven — where things come from, who made them

sampleCaptions: 50 years of treasures from around the world. Come dig through the racks.
Stewart is still at it — handcrafting one-of-a-kind pieces at 86.
New arrivals from our latest trip. Beads, buttons, and baubles.

commonPraise: Best prices for vintage in Toronto
Incredibly organized for a vintage shop
International treasures you won't find elsewhere
vibeDescriptors: organized chaos in the best way
feels like exploring a global market

contentStrategy.valueProposition: 50 years of handpicked vintage treasures, global beads, and one-of-a-kind jewelry in the heart of Kensington Market
keyMessages: Globally sourced — beads from Mexico, Indonesia, Egypt, Afghanistan
Three generations of the Scriver family curating treasures

imageStrategy.primarySubjects: products and store atmosphere — vintage clothing, beads, jewelry, storefront
imageStrategy.mood: warm and eclectic — colorful, layered, treasure-hunt feeling
imageStrategy.composition: mixed — close-ups of beads and jewelry, wide shots of packed racks and blue storefront

gallery.description: Vintage clothing rack — earth tones, tweeds, colorful jackets against red wall
Vintage teacups and fine china collection — floral patterns, gold rims
Colorful scarves and textiles — $10 bins, wicker baskets, blue wall
Blue storefront exterior — COURAGE MY LOVE blue awning, boots outside, mannequins on roof
Store listing hero image from blogTO

atmosphere.description: Cece Scriver inside the store, surrounded by jewelry cases and vintage clothing

people.name: Cece Scriver
people.bio: Daughter of founders Stewart and Patricia. Was 2 years old when the store opened in 1975. Now runs day-to-day operations with husband Owen.

people.name: Stewart Scriver
people.bio: Co-founded Courage My Love in 1975. Former special education teacher. At 86, still repairs and customizes vintage jewelry.

people.name: Patricia Scriver
people.bio: Co-founded Courage My Love in 1975. Had tenure at University of Toronto before leaving to pursue antiques and fashion.

people.name: Owen
people.bio: Former chef, now works at the store. Part of the third generation keeping the family business alive.

demographics.languageEvidence: All social media content in English. Google reviews in English. Kensington Market is multicultural but the store's primary customer base communicates in English. No consistent secondary language pattern found — customers come from all backgrounds (Chinese, Portuguese, Latin American, Caribbean communities all overlap in Kensington Market but the store does not serve any single non-English community predominantly).
demographics.neighborhood: Kensington Market — one of Toronto's most diverse, bohemian neighborhoods. Multicultural but English-dominant for commerce.
demographics.targetCustomer: Vintage enthusiasts, tourists exploring Kensington Market, creative types, students, treasure hunters, bead crafters, jewelry makers

archetype.rationale: Courage My Love is a visual treasure trove where the products ARE the experience — vintage clothing, beads, jewelry, antiques. The gallery of what's in store is the primary conversion driver. Visitors need to SEE the eclectic collection to feel the pull to visit.
